In [film](/wiki/Film "Film") and [television](/wiki/Television_show "Television show"), drama is a category or genre of [narrative](/wiki/Narrative "Narrative") [fiction](/wiki/Fiction "Fiction") (or [semi-fiction](/wiki/Docudrama "Docudrama")) intended to be more serious than [humorous](/wiki/Humour "Humour") in tone.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The drama of this kind is usually qualified with additional terms that specify its particular super-genre, macro-genre, or micro-genre,[[2]](#cite_note-Williams2017a-2) such as [soap opera](/wiki/Soap_opera "Soap opera"), [police crime drama](/wiki/Police_procedural "Police procedural"), [political drama](/wiki/Political_drama "Political drama"), [legal drama](/wiki/Legal_drama "Legal drama"), [historical drama](/wiki/Historical_drama "Historical drama"), [domestic drama](/wiki/Domestic_drama "Domestic drama"), [teen drama](#Teen_drama), and [comedy drama](/wiki/Comedy_drama "Comedy drama") (dramedy). These terms tend to indicate a particular [setting](/wiki/Setting_(narrative) "Setting (narrative)") or subject matter, or they combine a drama's otherwise serious tone with elements that encourage a broader range of [moods](/wiki/Mood_(literature) "Mood (literature)"). To these ends, a primary element in a drama is the occurrence of [conflict](/wiki/Conflict_(process) "Conflict (process)")—emotional, social, or otherwise—and its resolution in the course of the storyline.

All forms of [cinema](/wiki/Film_industry "Film industry") or television that involve [fictional stories](/wiki/Fiction "Fiction") are forms of [drama in the broader sense](/wiki/Drama "Drama") if their storytelling is achieved by means of actors who [represent](/wiki/Representation_(arts) "Representation (arts)") ([mimesis](/wiki/Mimesis "Mimesis")) [characters](/wiki/Character_(arts) "Character (arts)"). In this broader sense, drama is a [mode](/wiki/Mode_(literature) "Mode (literature)") distinct from novels, [short stories](/wiki/Short_story "Short story"), and narrative [poetry](/wiki/Poetry "Poetry") or [songs](/wiki/Song "Song").[[3]](#cite_note-elam98-3) In the [modern era](/wiki/Modern_era "Modern era"), before the birth of cinema or television, "drama" within [theatre](/wiki/Theatre "Theatre") was a type of [play](/wiki/Play_(theatre) "Play (theatre)") that was neither a [comedy](/wiki/Comedy_(drama) "Comedy (drama)") nor a [tragedy](/wiki/Tragedy "Tragedy"). It is this narrower sense that the film and television industries, along with [film studies](/wiki/Film_studies "Film studies"), adopted. "[Radio drama](/wiki/Radio_drama "Radio drama")" has been used in both senses—originally transmitted in a live performance, it has also been used to describe the more [highbrow](/wiki/Highbrow "Highbrow") and serious end of the dramatic output of [radio](/wiki/Radio "Radio").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Types of drama in film and television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Types of drama in film and television")]

|  |  |

The [Screenwriters Taxonomy](/wiki/Screenwriters_Taxonomy "Screenwriters Taxonomy") contends that film genres are fundamentally based upon a film's atmosphere, character and story, and therefore the labels "drama" and "comedy" are too broad to be considered a genre.[[2]](#cite_note-Williams2017a-2) Instead, the taxonomy contends that film dramas are a "Type" of film; listing at least ten different sub-types of film and television drama.[[5]](#cite_note-Williams2017b-5)

### Docudrama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Docudrama")]

Main article: [Docudrama](/wiki/Docudrama "Docudrama")

Docudramas are dramatized adaptations of real-life events. While not always completely accurate, the general facts are more-or-less true.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The difference between a docudrama and a documentary is that in a documentary it uses real people to describe history or current events; in a docudrama it uses professionally trained actors to play the roles in the current event, that is "dramatized" a bit. Examples: *[Black Mass](/wiki/Black_Mass_(film) "Black Mass (film)")* (2015) and *[Zodiac](/wiki/Zodiac_(film) "Zodiac (film)")* (2007).

### Docufiction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Docufiction")]

Main article: [Docufiction](/wiki/Docufiction "Docufiction")

Unlike docudramas, docu-fictional films combine documentary and fiction, where actual footage or real events are intermingled with recreated scenes.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Examples: *[Interior. Leather Bar](/wiki/Interior._Leather_Bar. "Interior. Leather Bar.")* (2013) and *[Your Name Here](/wiki/Your_Name_Here_(2015_film) "Your Name Here (2015 film)")* (2015).

### Comedy drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Comedy drama")]

Main article: [Comedy drama](/wiki/Comedy_drama "Comedy drama")

Many otherwise serious productions have humorous scenes and characters intended to provide [comic relief](/wiki/Comic_relief "Comic relief"). A comedy drama has humor as a more central component of the story, along with serious content.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Examples include *[Three Colours: White](/wiki/Three_Colours:_White "Three Colours: White")* (1994), *[The Truman Show](/wiki/The_Truman_Show "The Truman Show")* (1998), *[The Man Without a Past](/wiki/The_Man_Without_a_Past "The Man Without a Past")* (2002), *[The Best Exotic Marigold Hotel](/wiki/The_Best_Exotic_Marigold_Hotel "The Best Exotic Marigold Hotel")* (2011), and *[Silver Linings Playbook](/wiki/Silver_Linings_Playbook "Silver Linings Playbook")* (2012).

### Hypardrama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Hypardrama")]

Coined by film professor [Ken Dancyger](/wiki/Ken_Dancyger "Ken Dancyger"), these stories exaggerate characters and situations to the point of becoming fable, legend or fairy tale.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Examples: *[Fantastic Mr. Fox](/wiki/Fantastic_Mr._Fox_(film) "Fantastic Mr. Fox (film)")* (2009) and *[Maleficent](/wiki/Maleficent_(film) "Maleficent (film)")* (2014).

### Light drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Light drama")]

Light dramas are light-hearted stories that are, nevertheless, serious in nature.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Examples: *[The Help](/wiki/The_Help_(film) "The Help (film)")* (2011) and *[The Terminal](/wiki/The_Terminal "The Terminal")* (2004).

### Psychological drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Psychological drama")]

Main article: [Psychological drama](/wiki/Psychological_drama "Psychological drama")

Psychological dramas are dramas that focus on the characters' inner life and psychological problems.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Examples: *[Requiem for a Dream](/wiki/Requiem_for_a_Dream "Requiem for a Dream")* (2000), *[Oldboy](/wiki/Oldboy_(2003_film) "Oldboy (2003 film)")* (2003), *[Babel](/wiki/Babel_(film) "Babel (film)")* (2006), *[Whiplash](/wiki/Whiplash_(2014_film) "Whiplash (2014 film)")* (2014), and *[Anomalisa](/wiki/Anomalisa "Anomalisa")* (2015).

### Satirical drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Satirical drama")]

See also: [Satire (film and television)](/wiki/Satire_(film_and_television) "Satire (film and television)")

Satire can involve humor, but the result is typically sharp social commentary that is anything but funny. Satire often uses irony or exaggeration to expose faults in society or individuals that influence social ideology.[[12]](#cite_note-Williams2019-12) Examples: *[Thank You for Smoking](/wiki/Thank_You_for_Smoking "Thank You for Smoking")* (2005) and *[Idiocracy](/wiki/Idiocracy "Idiocracy")* (2006).

### Straight drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Straight drama")]

Straight drama applies to those that do not attempt a specific approach to drama but, rather, consider drama as a lack of comedic techniques.[[12]](#cite_note-Williams2019-12) Examples: *[Ghost World](/wiki/Ghost_World_(film) "Ghost World (film)")* (2001) and *[Wuthering Heights](/wiki/Wuthering_Heights_(2011_film) "Wuthering Heights (2011 film)")* (2011).

### Tragedy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Tragedy")]

Like most [tragedies](/wiki/Tragedy "Tragedy") in early [theatres](/wiki/Theatre "Theatre"), Tragedy typically focuses on the downfall or suffering of a main character (or sometimes supporting characters) due to a series of unfortunate events, often culminating in their demise at the end of the film or television series. Examples: *[The Elephant Man](/wiki/The_Elephant_Man_(1980_film) "The Elephant Man (1980 film)")* (1980), *[Grave of the Fireflies](/wiki/Grave_of_the_Fireflies "Grave of the Fireflies")* (1988), *[The Green Mile](/wiki/The_Green_Mile_(film) "The Green Mile (film)")* (1999), *[The Mist](/wiki/The_Mist_(film) "The Mist (film)")* (2007), and *[The Boy in the Striped Pyjamas](/wiki/The_Boy_in_the_Striped_Pyjamas_(film) "The Boy in the Striped Pyjamas (film)")* (2008).

## Type/genre combinations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Type/genre combinations")]

According to the Screenwriters' Taxonomy, all film descriptions should contain their type (comedy or drama) combined with one (or more) of the eleven super-genres.[[2]](#cite_note-Williams2017a-2) This combination does not create a separate genre, but rather, provides a better understanding of the film.

According to the taxonomy, combining the type with the genre does not create a separate genre.[[2]](#cite_note-Williams2017a-2) For instance, the "Horror Drama" is simply a dramatic horror film (as opposed to a comedic horror film). "Horror Drama" is not a genre separate from the horror genre or the drama type.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Crime drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Crime drama")]

Crime dramas explore themes of truth, justice, and freedom, and contain the fundamental dichotomy of "criminal vs. lawman". Crime films make the audience jump through a series of mental "hoops"; it is not uncommon for the crime drama to use verbal gymnastics to keep the audience and the protagonist on their toes.[[2]](#cite_note-Williams2017a-2)

Examples of crime dramas include: *[The Godfather](/wiki/The_Godfather "The Godfather")* (1972), *[Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown_(1974_film) "Chinatown (1974 film)")* (1974), *[Goodfellas](/wiki/Goodfellas "Goodfellas")* (1990), *[The Usual Suspects](/wiki/The_Usual_Suspects "The Usual Suspects")* (1995), and *[The Big Short](/wiki/The_Big_Short_(film) "The Big Short (film)")* (2015).

### Fantasy drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Fantasy drama ")]

According to [Eric R. Williams](/wiki/Eric_R._Williams "Eric R. Williams"), the hallmark of fantasy drama films is "a sense of wonderment, typically played out in a visually intense world inhabited by mythic creatures, magic or superhuman characters. Props and costumes within these films often belie a sense of mythology and folklore – whether ancient, futuristic, or other-worldly. The costumes, as well as the exotic world, reflect the personal, inner struggles that the hero faces in the story."[[2]](#cite_note-Williams2017a-2)

Examples of fantasy dramas include *[The Lord of the Rings](/wiki/The_Lord_of_the_Rings_(film_series) "The Lord of the Rings (film series)")* (2001–2003), *[Pan's Labyrinth](/wiki/Pan%27s_Labyrinth "Pan's Labyrinth")* (2006), *[Where the Wild Things Are](/wiki/Where_the_Wild_Things_Are_(film) "Where the Wild Things Are (film)")* (2009), and *[Life of Pi](/wiki/Life_of_Pi_(film) "Life of Pi (film)")* (2012).

### Horror drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Horror drama ")]

Horror dramas often involve the central characters isolated from the rest of society. These characters are often teenagers or people in their early twenties (the genre's central audience) and are eventually killed off during the course of the film. Thematically, horror films often serve as morality tales, with the killer serving up violent penance for the victims' past sins.[[5]](#cite_note-Williams2017b-5) Metaphorically, these become battles of Good vs. Evil or Purity vs. Sin.

*[Psycho](/wiki/Psycho_(1960_film) "Psycho (1960 film)")* (1960), *[The Shining](/wiki/The_Shining_(film) "The Shining (film)")* (1980), *[The Conjuring](/wiki/The_Conjuring "The Conjuring")* (2013), *[It](/wiki/It_(2017_film) "It (2017 film)")* (2017), *[mother!](/wiki/Mother! "Mother!")* (2017), and *[Hereditary](/wiki/Hereditary_(film) "Hereditary (film)")* (2018) are examples of horror drama films.

### Life drama (day-in-the-life)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Life drama (day-in-the-life)")]

Further information: [Slice of life](/wiki/Slice_of_life "Slice of life")

Day-in-the-life films takes small events in a person's life and raises their level of importance. The "small things in life" feel as important to the protagonist (and the audience) as the climactic battle in an action film, or the final shootout in a western.[[5]](#cite_note-Williams2017b-5) Often, the protagonists deal with multiple, overlapping issues in the course of the film – just as we do in life.

Films of this type/genre combination include: *[The Wrestler](/wiki/The_Wrestler_(2008_film) "The Wrestler (2008 film)")* (2008), *[Fruitvale Station](/wiki/Fruitvale_Station "Fruitvale Station")* (2013), *[Locke](/wiki/Locke_(film) "Locke (film)")* (2013), *[The Great Beauty](/wiki/The_Great_Beauty "The Great Beauty")* (2013) and *[The Last Beautiful Thing](/wiki/The_Last_Beautiful_Thing "The Last Beautiful Thing")* (2025).

### Romantic drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Romantic drama")]

Romantic dramas are films with central themes that reinforce our beliefs about love (e.g.: themes such as "love at first sight", "love conquers all", or "there is someone out there for everyone"); the story typically revolves around characters falling into (and out of, and back into) love.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

*[Annie Hall](/wiki/Annie_Hall "Annie Hall")* (1977), *[The Notebook](/wiki/The_Notebook "The Notebook")* (2004), *[Carol](/wiki/Carol_(film) "Carol (film)")* (2015), *[Her](/wiki/Her_(2013_film) "Her (2013 film)")* (2013)*, [The Great Gatsby](/wiki/The_Great_Gatsby_(2013_film) "The Great Gatsby (2013 film)")* (2013) *and [La La Land](/wiki/La_La_Land "La La Land")* (2016) are examples of romance dramas.

### Science fiction drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Science fiction drama")]

The science fiction drama film is often the story of a protagonist (and their allies) facing something "unknown" that has the potential to change the future of humanity; this unknown may be represented by a villain with incomprehensible powers, a creature we do not understand, or a scientific scenario that threatens to change the world; the science fiction story forces the audience to consider the nature of human beings, the confines of time or space or the concepts of human existence in general.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Examples include: *[Metropolis](/wiki/Metropolis_(1927_film) "Metropolis (1927 film)")* (1927), *[Planet of the Apes](/wiki/Planet_of_the_Apes_(1968_film) "Planet of the Apes (1968 film)")* (1968), *[A Clockwork Orange](/wiki/A_Clockwork_Orange_(film) "A Clockwork Orange (film)")* (1971), *[Blade Runner](/wiki/Blade_Runner "Blade Runner")* (1982) and its sequel *[Blade Runner 2049](/wiki/Blade_Runner_2049 "Blade Runner 2049")* (2017), *[Children of Men](/wiki/Children_of_Men "Children of Men")* (2006), *[Interstellar](/wiki/Interstellar_(film) "Interstellar (film)")* (2014), and *[Arrival](/wiki/Arrival_(film) "Arrival (film)")* (2016).

### Sports drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Sports drama")]

Further information: [Sports film](/wiki/Sports_film "Sports film")

In the sports super-genre, characters will be playing sports. Thematically, the story is often one of "Our Team" versus "Their Team"; their team will always try to win, and our team will show the world that they deserve recognition or redemption; the story does not always have to involve a team. The story could also be about an individual athlete or the story could focus on an individual playing on a team.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Examples of this genre/type include: *[The Hustler](/wiki/The_Hustler "The Hustler")* (1961), *[Hoosiers](/wiki/Hoosiers_(film) "Hoosiers (film)")* (1986), *[Remember the Titans](/wiki/Remember_the_Titans "Remember the Titans")* (2000), and *[Moneyball](/wiki/Moneyball_(film) "Moneyball (film)")* (2011).

### War drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: War drama")]

Further information: [War film](/wiki/War_film "War film")

War films typically tells the story of a small group of isolated individuals who – one by one – get killed (literally or metaphorically) by an outside force until there is a final fight to the death; the idea of the protagonists facing death is a central expectation in a war drama film. In a war film even though the enemy may out-number, or out-power, the hero, we assume that the enemy *can* be defeated if only the hero can figure out how.[[5]](#cite_note-Williams2017b-5)

Examples include: *[Apocalypse Now](/wiki/Apocalypse_Now "Apocalypse Now")* (1979), *[Come and See](/wiki/Come_and_See "Come and See")* (1985), *[Braveheart](/wiki/Braveheart "Braveheart")* (1995), *[Life Is Beautiful](/wiki/Life_Is_Beautiful "Life Is Beautiful")* (1997), *[Black Book](/wiki/Black_Book_(film) "Black Book (film)")* (2006), *[The Hurt Locker](/wiki/The_Hurt_Locker "The Hurt Locker")* (2008), *[1944](/wiki/1944_(film) "1944 (film)")* (2015), *[Wildeye](/wiki/Wildeye "Wildeye")* (2015), and *[1917](/wiki/1917_(2019_film) "1917 (2019 film)")* (2019).

### Western drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Western drama ")]

Films in the [western](/wiki/Western_(genre) "Western (genre)") super-genre often take place in the [American Southwest](/wiki/American_Southwest "American Southwest") or Mexico, with a large number of scenes occurring outdoors so we can soak in scenic landscapes. Visceral expectations for the audience include fistfights, gunplay, and chase scenes. There is also the expectation of spectacular panoramic images of the countryside including sunsets, wide open landscapes, and endless deserts and sky.[[2]](#cite_note-Williams2017a-2)

Examples of western dramas include: *[True Grit](/wiki/True_Grit_(1969_film) "True Grit (1969 film)")* (1969) and its [2010 remake](/wiki/True_Grit_(2010_film) "True Grit (2010 film)"), *[Mad Max](/wiki/Mad_Max_(film) "Mad Max (film)")* (1979), *[Unforgiven](/wiki/Unforgiven "Unforgiven")* (1992), *[No Country for Old Men](/wiki/No_Country_for_Old_Men "No Country for Old Men")* (2007), *[Django Unchained](/wiki/Django_Unchained "Django Unchained")* (2012), *[Hell or High Water](/wiki/Hell_or_High_Water_(film) "Hell or High Water (film)")* (2016), and *[Logan](/wiki/Logan_(film) "Logan (film)")* (2017).

## Misidentified categories

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Misidentified categories")]

Some film categories that use the word "comedy" or "drama" are not recognized by the Screenwriters Taxonomy as either a film genre or a film type. For instance, "Melodrama" and "Screwball Comedy" are considered pathways,[[17]](#cite_note-17) while "romantic comedy" and "family drama" are macro-genres.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Family drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Family drama")]

A macro-genre in the Screenwriters Taxonomy. These films tell a story in which many of the central characters are related. The story revolves around how the family as a whole reacts to a central challenge. There are four micro-genres for the family drama: *Family Bond*, *Family Feud*, *Family Loss*, and *Family Rift*.[[2]](#cite_note-Williams2017a-2)

### Melodrama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Melodrama")]

Main article: [Melodrama](/wiki/Melodrama "Melodrama")

A sub-type of drama films that uses plots that appeal to the heightened emotions of the audience. Melodramatic plots often deal with "crises of human emotion, failed romance or friendship, strained familial situations, tragedy, illness, neuroses, or emotional and physical hardship".[[19]](#cite_note-19) Film critics sometimes use the term "pejoratively to connote an unrealistic, pathos-filled, [camp](/wiki/Camp_(style) "Camp (style)") tale of romance or domestic situations with stereotypical characters (often including a central female character) that would directly appeal to feminine audiences".[[20]](#cite_note-20) Also called "women's movies", "weepies", tearjerkers, or "chick flicks". If they are targeted to a male audience, then they are called "guy cry" films. Often considered "soap-opera" drama.

### Liturgical drama / religious drama / Christian drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Liturgical drama / religious drama / Christian drama")]

See also: [Liturgical drama](/wiki/Liturgical_drama "Liturgical drama") and [Christian drama](/wiki/Christian_drama "Christian drama")

Focuses on religious characters, mystery play, beliefs, and respect.

### Crime drama / police procedural / legal drama / courtroom drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Crime drama / police procedural / legal drama / courtroom drama")]

See also: [Crime drama](/wiki/Crime_drama "Crime drama"), [police procedural](/wiki/Police_procedural "Police procedural"), and [legal drama](/wiki/Legal_drama "Legal drama")

Character development based on themes involving criminals, law enforcement and the legal system.

### Historical drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Historical drama")]

Main article: [Historical drama](/wiki/Historical_drama "Historical drama")

Films that focus on dramatic events in history.

### Medical drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Medical drama")]

Main article: [Medical drama](/wiki/Medical_drama "Medical drama")

Focuses on doctors, nurses, hospital staff, and ambulance saving victims and the interactions of their daily lives.

### Teen drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drama_(film_and_television)&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Teen drama")]

See also: [List of teen dramas](/wiki/List_of_teen_dramas "List of teen dramas")

Focuses on [teenage](/wiki/Teenage "Teenage") characters.
